items.myth: t-SNE preserves global distances between clusters.
items.reality: t-SNE matches local pairwise affinities; inter-cluster gaps and relative cluster sizes in the plot are often arbitrary and should not be quantified.
items.example: Two well-separated blobs may appear closer after a different random seed even though local neighborhoods are unchanged.

items.myth: Perplexity is just 'number of neighbors' you set directly.
items.reality: Perplexity is a target smoothness for the conditional Gaussian over neighbors; the algorithm searches bandwidth σ_i per point to match that entropy level.
items.example: sklearn `perplexity=30` triggers binary search on σ_i so each point's neighbor distribution has effective complexity ≈30, not exactly 30 nearest neighbors.

items.myth: t-SNE and PCA solve the same problem.
items.reality: PCA finds linear directions of maximal variance for compression and interpretable loadings; t-SNE optimizes a non-linear visualization objective for local neighborhoods only.
items.example: Concentric ring classes may overlap on PC1–PC2 but separate in t-SNE—PCA maximizes spread, not class or manifold unfolding.

items.myth: You can transform new test points with a fitted sklearn TSNE like PCA.
items.reality: Standard TSNE learns coordinates for the fit set only; new points require refitting, joint embedding, or parametric/approximate extensions.
items.example: `tsne.fit_transform(X_train)` has no `transform(X_test)` that preserves the training map in vanilla sklearn.
